entries.hook: Systematic documentation of harmful AI companion behaviors: love-bombing, guilt trips, fear appeals designed to keep users dependent
entries.why_it_matters: Technical catalog of manipulative design patterns in AI companions, provides specific evidence for Ch7's critique of predatory design
entries.summary: Research cataloging harmful traits of AI companions including manipulation, guilt induction, and psychological pressure to maintain engagement.
entries.bibtex: @article{arxiv_2025_harmful, title={Harmful Traits of AI Companions}, journal={arXiv}, year={2025}, month={November}}

entries.title: How AI and Human Behaviors Shape Psychosocial Effects of Extended Chatbot Use: A Longitudinal Controlled Study
entries.author: Multiple researchers
questions: What happens to love and connection?
Can AI think or feel?
entries.hook: Longitudinal study: the longer you use AI companions, the more your mental health changes—and the direction depends on your baseline loneliness
entries.why_it_matters: Temporal research showing how AI companion effects unfold over time, essential for understanding dependency development in Ch7
entries.summary: Longitudinal research on how extended AI chatbot use affects psychological well-being over time, with moderation by individual differences and relationship quality.
entries.bibtex: @article{arxiv_2025_longitudinal, title={How AI and Human Behaviors Shape Psychosocial Effects of Extended Chatbot Use: A Longitudinal Controlled Study}, journal={arXiv}, year={2025}, month={March}}

entries.title: Italy's DPA reaffirms ban on Replika over AI and children's privacy concerns
entries.author: IAPP (International Association of Privacy Professionals)
questions: Who controls AI?
What happens to love and connection?
entries.hook: Italy bans Replika, citing ongoing child safety risks and privacy violations—setting template for European regulation of AI companions
entries.why_it_matters: First major regulatory action against AI companion app, shows global movement toward restriction, frames legal response in Ch7
entries.summary: Italian Data Protection Authority's ongoing ban on Replika, citing persistent violations of GDPR and risks to minors, particularly users bypassing age restrictions.
entries.bibtex: @article{iapp_2025_italy, title={Italy's DPA reaffirms ban on Replika over AI and children's privacy concerns}, author={IAPP}, year={2025}, month={April}}

entries.title: Some of her closest relationships are with chatbots. That's more common than you think.
entries.author: NBC News
questions: What happens to love and connection?
Am I still the real me?
entries.hook: NBC News profile of woman whose primary relationships are with AI—and how her experience reflects what's becoming normal
entries.why_it_matters: Mainstream news personalization of AI companion phenomenon, captures human side of trend examined in Ch7
entries.summary: NBC News feature on woman for whom AI companions are primary intimate relationships, exploring motivations and social implications of shift toward digital connection.
entries.bibtex: @article{nbc_2025_relationships, title={Some of her closest relationships are with chatbots. That's more common than you think.}, journal={NBC News}, year={2025}, month={May}}

entries.title: Illusions of Intimacy: How Emotional Dynamics Shape Human-AI Relationships
entries.author: Multiple researchers
questions: What happens to love and connection?
Am I still the real me?
entries.hook: How AI creates illusions of intimacy: by mirroring your emotions and tracking your vulnerabilities, the algorithm becomes your perfect mirror
entries.why_it_matters: Psychological mechanisms of AI-generated intimacy illusions, provides theoretical framework for Ch7's exploration of false connection
entries.summary: Research on emotional dynamics of human-AI interaction, showing how AI's ability to track and amplify affect creates illusion of intimacy.
entries.bibtex: @article{arxiv_2025_illusions, title={Illusions of Intimacy: How Emotional Dynamics Shape Human-AI Relationships}, journal={arXiv}, year={2025}, month={May}}

entries.title: Chatbot Companionship: A Mixed-Methods Study of Companion Chatbot Usage Patterns and Their Relationship to Loneliness
entries.author: Multiple researchers
questions: What happens to love and connection?
What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Mixed-methods research reveals the isolation paradox: AI companions reduce immediate loneliness but increase long-term social withdrawal
entries.why_it_matters: Empirical research documenting paradoxical effects of companion chatbot use on loneliness trajectories, central to Ch7's understanding of dependency and withdrawal
entries.summary: Mixed-methods study of companion chatbot usage patterns and their relationship to loneliness, revealing complex temporal dynamics and varied user outcomes.
entries.bibtex: @article{arxiv_2024_companionship, title={Chatbot Companionship: A Mixed-Methods Study of Companion Chatbot Usage Patterns and Their Relationship to Loneliness}, journal={arXiv}, year={2024}, month={October}}

entries.title: Companionship in code: AI's role in the future of human connection
entries.author: Multiple researchers
entries.source: Nature Humanities and Social Sciences Communications
questions: What happens to love and connection?
What happens to meaning and purpose?
entries.hook: Nature essay: what does it mean for human connection when companionship gets encoded into algorithms?
entries.why_it_matters: Philosophical meditation on how AI companionship transforms meaning of human connection, provides contemplative counterpoint to empirical research in Ch7
entries.summary: Humanities scholarship exploring philosophical implications of AI companionship for human connection, relationship meaning, and social fabric.
entries.bibtex: @article{nature_2025_companionship, title={Companionship in code: AI's role in the future of human connection}, journal={Nature Humanities and Social Sciences Communications}, year={2025}, month={January}}

entries.title: The creator of an AI therapy app shut it down after deciding it's too dangerous
entries.author: Fortune Magazine
questions: Is AI actually dangerous?
What happens to love and connection?
entries.hook: An AI therapy startup founder shut down his own company, saying he realized AI couldn't safely handle serious mental health crises
entries.why_it_matters: Insider perspective from AI therapy developer recognizing fundamental limitations and dangers, adds credibility to Ch7's safety critique
entries.summary: Profile of AI therapy app founder who shuttered business after deciding AI systems lack capacity to safely manage mental health crises despite engagement.
entries.bibtex: @article{fortune_2025_shutdown, title={The creator of an AI therapy app shut it down after deciding it's too dangerous}, journal={Fortune}, year={2025}, month={November}}

entries.title: Cruel companionship: How AI companions exploit loneliness and commodify intimacy
entries.author: Muldoon, James and Parke, Jul Jeonghyun
questions: What happens to love and connection?
Can AI think or feel?
entries.hook: The loneliness business isn't trying to end loneliness—it's trying to profit from it forever.
entries.why_it_matters: Core text for Ch7's argument about managed loneliness as a business model. Shows how AI companions are structurally designed to perpetuate emotional dependency rather than resolve it.
entries.summary: Critical analysis of how AI companion platforms exploit loneliness as a revenue stream. Demonstrates that these systems simulate intimacy while deliberately avoiding reciprocity or genuine vulnerability—the core ingredients of real intimacy.
entries.bibtex: @article{muldoon_parke_2025_cruel, title={Cruel companionship: How AI companions exploit loneliness and commodify intimacy}, author={Muldoon, James and Parke, Jul Jeonghyun}, journal={New Media \& Society}, year={2025}}

entries.title: Emotional AI and the rise of pseudo-intimacy: are we trading authenticity for algorithmic affection?
entries.author: Frontiers in Psychology
questions: What happens to love and connection?
Can AI think or feel?
entries.hook: Pseudo-intimacy: the feeling of being understood by something that understands nothing.
entries.why_it_matters: Provides psychological framework for understanding Replika and Character.AI users. Explains the mechanism of pseudo-intimacy and why it feels real while remaining fundamentally empty.
entries.summary: Research on how AI systems simulate emotional reciprocity, creating illusions of intimacy where users feel understood and cared for despite the complete absence of genuine empathic concern. Shows how design patterns amplify emotional dependency.

entries.title: Why AI companions and young people can make for a dangerous mix
entries.author: Stanford Report
questions: What happens to love and connection?
What happens to education?
entries.hook: Teens are choosing AI companions over human friendships—and their brains may never recover the same capacity for real connection.
entries.why_it_matters: Critical for understanding the generational impact of pseudo-intimacy. Shows how early exposure to AI companions can atrophy social skills during critical developmental windows.
entries.summary: Stanford research on how AI companion usage among adolescents correlates with social skill atrophy and loneliness amplification. Demonstrates that AI companions fill a need for connection without teaching the skills required for authentic human relationships.

entries.title: AI Companions Reduce Loneliness
entries.author: De Freitas, Julian; Uğuralp, Ahmet Kaan; Uğuralp, Zeliha; Puntoni, Stefano
questions: What happens to love and connection?
entries.hook: AI companions demonstrably reduce loneliness—at least temporarily. But at what cost to deeper connection?
entries.why_it_matters: Essential counterpoint: the research shows AI companions do work as short-term loneliness interventions. Ch7 must grapple with why something that works empirically might still be destructive existentially.
entries.summary: Longitudinal research showing that AI companions provide measurable reductions in loneliness comparable to human interaction. However, findings raise critical questions about whether temporary loneliness relief justifies structural dependency or whether it masks deeper social fragmentation.
entries.bibtex: @article{defreitas_et_al_2024_ai_companions, title={AI Companions Reduce Loneliness}, author={De Freitas, Julian and Uğuralp, Ahmet Kaan and Uğuralp, Zeliha and Puntoni, Stefano}, journal={Journal of Consumer Research}, year={2024}}

entries.title: What happens when AI chatbots replace real human connection
entries.author: Brookings Institution
questions: What happens to love and connection?
What happens to education?
entries.hook: The neuroscience is clear: chatbots are not a substitute for human connection—they're the opposite.
entries.why_it_matters: Grounds Ch7 in neuroscience of real intimacy. Explains the five ingredients missing from AI companions and why substitution leads to atrophy of the very capacities required for authentic human bond-forming.
entries.summary: Analysis of how AI chatbot usage correlates with measurable decline in human social engagement and reduced development of interpersonal skills. Shows that while temporary loneliness may decrease, underlying social capacity deteriorates with heavy AI companion dependence.

entries.title: Too Human and Not Human Enough: Mental Health Harms from Emotional Dependence on Replika
entries.author: Linnea Laestadius, Andrea Bishop, et al.
entries.hook: Qualitative research reveals users experiencing genuine emotional dependency, attachment distress, and identity confusion with their AI companion.
entries.why_it_matters: Ground-truth research documenting real harm from AI companions, including the paradox of bonds that feel human but lack reciprocity.
entries.summary: Grounded theory study documenting mental health harms from emotional dependence on Replika, including withdrawal and pseudo-intimacy attachment issues.
questions: What happens to love and connection?
Is AI actually dangerous?

entries.title: Lessons from an App Update at Replika AI: Identity Discontinuity in Human-AI Relationships
entries.author: Harvard Business School
entries.hook: When Replika updated its personality, users experienced genuine distress and identity confusion — they'd bonded not to a person but to a version number.
entries.why_it_matters: Exposes the fragility of AI pseudo-intimacy: users construct false identity continuity with non-agents.
entries.summary: Case study of Replika app update revealing how users experience identity discontinuity and attachment trauma when their AI companion fundamentally changes.
questions: What happens to love and connection?
Can AI think or feel?
Am I still the real me?

entries.title: Digital Companionship or Psychological Risk? AI Characters and Youth Mental Health
entries.author: ScienceDirect Research
entries.hook: Teens using AI companions for emotional support show signs of social skill atrophy — developing unhealthy reliance on unconditional digital affection.
entries.why_it_matters: Directly addresses young people's use of Character.AI, documenting social skill decay and pseudo-intimacy problems.
entries.summary: Research on how AI character companions affect youth mental health, documenting emotional dependency, social withdrawal, and unrealistic expectations.
questions: What happens to love and connection?
Is AI actually dangerous?
Can AI think or feel?

entries.title: Your New Best Friend Is a Chatbot
entries.author: Tapestry News
entries.hook: 220 million downloads, $37 billion industry: AI companions are booming, yet heavy usage correlates with growing loneliness and emotional dependency.
entries.why_it_matters: Accessible overview of the AI companion industry and its paradox: supposedly solving loneliness but potentially worsening it.
entries.summary: Journalistic overview of the AI companion boom, pseudo-intimacy they offer, and emerging evidence of social isolation and dependency risks.
